title: Installing permeable paving on drives and garden paths
subtitle: Choosing and laying permeable surfaces to reduce surface runoff and comply with UK planning rules
excerpt: Replacing an impermeable drive or path with a permeable surface lets rainwater soak through rather than running off to the sewer. In England, paving over more than 5 m2 of front garden with an impermeable surface requires planning permission; permeable paving does not. This covers the main surface types, sub-base requirements, and installation sequence for gravel, resin-bound, and permeable block paving.
sourceNotes: DCLG (now DLUHC) permitted development guidance for front garden paving. CIRIA SuDS manual guidance on permeable paving. BS 7533-13:2009 guidance on permeable block paving. Environment Agency surface water drainage guidance. RHS guidance on gravel and resin-bound paths.

glossaryTerms.definition: Sustainable Drainage Systems. A range of techniques that mimic natural drainage by allowing water to infiltrate, evaporate, or flow slowly rather than discharging rapidly to the sewer network.
Crushed rock sub-base material graded to allow compaction into a stable load-bearing layer. Used under paving, driveways, and paths. Available from builders merchants in bulk bags or loose loads.

content.text: Impermeable drives and paths send rainwater straight to the sewer, contributing to flooding during heavy rain. Permeable paving is a
SuDS

technique: water passes through the surface into a permeable sub-base, then soaks into the ground or drains to a soakaway. There is also a planning benefit in England. A drive over 5 m2 using an impermeable surface needs planning permission. The same drive in any permeable material does not.
Surface type comparison

Gravel (loose or bound): lowest installation cost. Permeable by nature. Loose gravel migrates underfoot and vehicle tyres; bound gravel (resin or tar) stays in place but reduces permeability. Suitable for paths and lightly trafficked areas.

Resin-bound aggregate: aggregate stones set in a clear UV-stable resin on a permeable base. Smooth surface, aesthetically close to tarmac. Suitable for drives. Requires professional application for larger areas; DIY kits available for paths up to 10 m2.
Permeable block paving: concrete or clay blocks with permeable jointing material (not mortar). Water passes through the joints into a permeable sub-base below. Most DIY-friendly of the hard surfaces.
Cellular paving (grass or gravel grid): plastic honeycomb grids filled with gravel or turf. Maximum permeability. Suitable for infrequently used parking areas.
Sub-base and installation sequence
For permeable block paving on a drive, the sub-base must also be permeable. Conventional dense
Type 1 aggregate
compacted tightly is not appropriate; use open-graded Type 3 sub-base or a single-size clean gravel in the 20-40 mm range.
Excavate to the required depth: 200-250 mm for a driveway, 100-150 mm for a path. Check for any underground services before digging.
Lay a geotextile membrane on the compacted sub-grade to prevent soil migration into the aggregate.
Place the permeable sub-base (Type 3 or open-graded aggregate) to within 50-80 mm of finished level. Compact lightly; do not over-compact open-graded material.
Lay a 30-40 mm layer of coarse angular chippings as a bedding course for blocks, or proceed directly to gravel infill for cellular paving.
Install edge restraints (concrete haunching or polymer edge strips) before laying blocks to prevent lateral spread.
Lay blocks or paving in the chosen pattern. Fill joints with 2-5 mm clean single-size angular grit (not building sand, which clogs and prevents drainage). Brush dry grit into joints and compact.
Maintenance
Weed control: permeable surfaces support weed growth in joints. Treat annually with a path weedkiller or use a propane weed burner on joints.
Jet washing: clears blocked joints but also flushes out the drainage grit. Replenish joint grit after jet washing.
Moss and algae: appear in shaded areas. Wire brush or pressure wash; avoid algaecides on permeable surfaces as residues can affect the underlying soakaway biology.

attrs.symptom: Puddles forming on the permeable surface after heavy rain
attrs.cause: Joints are blocked with fine particles (dust, organic material) reducing the infiltration rate
attrs.fix: Jet wash the surface, then replenish the joint grit. If blocking recurs quickly, the catchment area may be bringing in too much silt; add a leaf trap or gully at the high end of the run.

attrs.symptom: Blocks are sinking or becoming uneven after the first winter
attrs.cause: Sub-base was insufficiently compacted, or the open-graded aggregate is washing through the geotextile into the sub-grade
attrs.fix: Lift the affected blocks, add aggregate to build back the level, compact gently, relay blocks, and refresh joint grit. Check that the geotextile membrane is intact below the sub-base.
